text: This video demonstrates the use of a homemade, inexpensive and easily constructed hand port to facilitate laparoscopic segmental sigmoid colon resection. The patient is a fifty nine year old male with a body mass index of thirty two kilograms per meter squared. Eight weeks prior he underwent a combined endolaparoscopic resection of a ten millimeter sessile sigmoid colon polyp. Pathology showed the polyp to contain a two millimeter poorly differentiated invasive cancer with clear resection margins. The patient consented to proceed with segmental sigmoid colon resection to stage and treat potential nodal involvement.

The laparoscope reveals challenges in exposing the sigmoid mesentery due to a fatty and short small bowel mesentery. The patient is positioned in steep Trendelenburg with the left side elevated, but this provides minimal displacement of the small bowel. The tattoo marking the polypectomy site is visible on the colon. Attempts to reposition the omentum over the liver and flip small bowel loops kephalad offer little additional exposure. Large appendices epiploicae on the sigmoid colon further hinder exposure. The surgeon abandons medial to lateral dissection due to poor exposure and instead performs lateral mobilization of the sigmoid colon. The left ureter is identified and protected. The distal transection level is marked at the pelvic brim, and a window is created in the mesentery next to the bowel, which is then divided at this level.

The surgeon constructs a homemade hand port using a wound retractor and a glove to improve exposure of the sigmoid mesentery. A small incision is made, and the wound retractor is inserted and tightened. The surgeon's right hand is placed in the abdomen, allowing for hand assistance to expose, dissect, and divide the sigmoidal branches despite the presence of small bowel loops. The sigmoid colon is exteriorized and transected through the incision. The anvil of a 31mm circular stapler is sewn in with a purse-string suture, and a stapled end-to-end colorectal anastomosis is performed after re-insufflating pneumoperitoneum.
The surgeon performs an air leak test on the colorectal anastomosis, confirming it is airtight.
